Fun Fact: Precious (2009)
Precious: Based on the Novel âPushâ by Sapphire was one of the most powerful independent films of its decade, and its road to success was remarkable. The film premiered at the Sundance Film Festival in 2009, where it won both the Grand Jury Prize and the Audience Award â a rare double win that immediately drew Hollywoodâs attention. It went on to receive six Academy Award nominations, ultimately winning two (Best Supporting Actress for MoâNique and Best Adapted Screenplay for Geoffrey Fletcher, who became the first African-American writer to win in that category).

Behind the scenes, the movie had unlikely champions. Producers Oprah Winfrey and Tyler Perry lent their names and influence to the project after seeing early cuts of the film, helping it gain wider distribution and visibility. Director Lee Daniels pushed for a raw and uncompromising portrayal of the story, insisting on shooting in real Harlem locations to preserve authenticity.

The casting of Gabourey Sidibe as Claireece âPreciousâ Jones was itself extraordinary. Sidibe was a college student with no professional acting experience when she auditioned. Her emotional depth and vulnerability stunned Daniels, and her performance became one of the most celebrated acting debuts in modern cinema. Critics praised how she and MoâNique (playing Preciousâs abusive mother) created one of the most intense and unforgettable on-screen dynamics ever captured in a drama.

Financially, the film was also a surprise hit. Made on a modest budget of around $10 million, it grossed over $63 million worldwide, proving that deeply personal and challenging stories could find a mainstream audience. Today, Precious is remembered not only as an artistic achievement but also as a milestone for representation in American cinema.

Have you ever wondered why a snake constantly flicks its tongue in and out? It is not a threat or a habit â it is how a snake âseesâ the world.

A snakeâs tongue is a powerful sensory tool. When it flicks its tongue, it collects microscopic scent particles from the air and the ground. These particles are then transferred to a special organ in the roof of the snakeâs mouth called the Jacobsonâs organ. This allows the snake to analyze smells with incredible precision.

By doing this, a snake can determine the direction of prey, detect predators, recognize other snakes, and even understand how recently another animal passed through the area. The faster and more frequently the tongue flicks, the more information the snake is gathering.

In a way, a snake does not just smell its surroundings â it reads them. The tongue is its guide, helping it survive in a world where senses must be sharp and every movement matters.

#ð¯ðµ People's footsteps are being converted into electricity in Japan
In Japan, special piezoceramic tiles are used to generate electricity from the pressure of people's footsteps. When you step on them, the tiles bend, creating a mechanical tension that the piezoelectric material converts into electricity.
About 2.4 million people contribute to this system at Shibuya Station every day.
The energy generated is used to power LED lights, displays, and sensors. This method is very useful for sustainable energy and modern urban infrastructure.
